text: The surgeon performs a robotic tap ventral hernia repair, creating a preperitoneal pocket. The surgical field shows a prior midline laparotomy with a visible ventral hernia defect. The surgeon checks for bladder involvement in the hernia and ensures the bladder is decompressed. Dissection continues beneath the posterior sheath below the arcuate line, with a small bleeder from a branch of the inferior epigastric artery being controlled.

The surgeon approaches the hernia defect, with the rectus abdominis muscle visible laterally to the defect on the linea alba. Careful dissection is performed to avoid dividing the muscle, and the hernia sac is skeletonized, creating a 'volcano sign.' The surgeon reduces the fat around the hernia, ensuring the bladder is not involved, as only fat is visible.

The surgeon divides the attachments of the hernia sac and reduces the hernia contents, primarily consisting of fat. The dissection continues down to the pubic tubercle. The hernia defect is measured, revealing dimensions of fifteen by twelve centimeters with a three-centimeter defect. A V-Lock suture is used to close the defect, with sequential throws applied before tightening due to the thin muscle.

The surgeon tightens the closure of the defect, carefully distributing tension to avoid tearing the thin rectus abdominis muscle fibers. The pneumo pressure is lowered to ten for better handling of larger defects. A Progrip mesh is placed over the defect, adhering to the abdominal wall without sutures, covering the midline and extending laterally.

The surgeon uses a 3 LV lock suture to close the peritoneal flap over the mesh, ensuring the hernia is completely reduced with no visible holes in the hernia sac. The mesh is fully covered by the flap.
